front: Building: sits on Bennelong Point on Sydney Harbor; roof of fourteen interlocking sail-like white shells
Building: shells cast as sections of a sphere — 'spherical solution,' apocryphally inspired by peeling an orange; engineered by Ove Arup and Co.
Building: ran 1300% over budget; Utzon resigned in 1966 before completion; Peter Hall oversaw completion; architect was neither invited nor mentioned at the 1973 opening
Building: design famously pulled from a pile of rejected submissions by juror Eero Saarinen
Building: contains the Joan Sutherland Theater; outer shells covered in more than a million white tiles
Building: Le Corbusier's tapestry 'The Dice Are Cast' was commissioned for it, scrapped by Peter Hall, but eventually acquired; one room was later renamed for the architect
Building: the Dennis Wolanski library and archives are located here; 100-meter-wide stairs designed to instill a feeling of 'otherworldliness'
Building: designed with son Jan; combines a flat-roofed mosque with a public square topped by an inclined roof rising toward the Persian Gulf; inspired by a visit to Isfahan
Building: set on fire by retreating Iraqi troops during the Gulf War
Building: all-white naturally lit interior; vaulted ceiling of reinforced concrete shells inspired by a Chinese pagoda roof or a line of clouds
Building: cited by critic Kenneth Frampton to introduce the concept of 'Critical Regionalism'
Architect: Danish architect; won the Pritzker Prize in 2003
Architect: 'additive architecture' — buildings as the sum of repeating constitutive elements, inspired by nature's growth principles including Chinese temple design
Architect: L-shaped Kingo Houses — courtyard dwellings along cul-de-sacs in Denmark; inspired Bjarke Ingels's Mountain Dwellings; also designed his own house in Hellebaek
Architect: Svaneke Water Tower — spiral staircase ascends into a pyramid resting on three ferro-concrete legs; a planned stadium in Jeddah also exemplifies his additive architecture
